All solutions

Color schemes

Sixteen palettes, each one shown on a real design

Hex codes are easy to find. What's hard is knowing how a scheme feels once it's a whole website. So every palette here comes with one — a full page laid out in exactly those colors.

Counsel Navy & Brass

Law · Finance · Wealth management

dark
BG #0B1A2B
Surface #12263A
Text #EAF1F8
Muted #9DB2C6
Border #1E3A52
Primary #C8A24A
Accent #4F86B8

Deep navy reads as institutional and settled; the brass accent adds age and authority without looking gilded. The safest choice when the buying decision is trust.

whitlockhale.com
Whitlock & Hale Practice AreasAttorneysResultsContact Free consultation

Four decades of courtroom results for businesses and families across the state.

A warm paper background with a single deep red. Feels like letterpress stationery — premium and personal rather than corporate.

Borrowed straight from the jobsite. High-visibility orange on cold steel greys signals capability and scale — and it survives being seen on a phone in daylight.

Cool, bright, and clean without going sterile. The mint accent is the reassurance color — use it for confirmations, availability, and anything that lowers anxiety.

Aggressive and modern. Lime on charcoal has enormous contrast, so keep it to buttons, numbers, and rules — a lime paragraph is unreadable.

Every preview above is live HTML, not a screenshot — the same eight tokens that theme it are the ones your site would ship with.

Choosing well

What makes a scheme work

Four rules behind every palette on this page. They're worth knowing even if you build the site yourself.

The 60 / 30 / 10 split

Sixty percent background, thirty percent surfaces and text, ten percent accent. Every scheme below follows it — that ratio is why one accent color can carry a whole site without shouting.

Contrast is not optional

Body text holds at least 4.5:1 against its background and buttons hold 3:1 against theirs. A palette that fails this is a redesign in twelve months, not a style choice.

One accent, used sparingly

The accent belongs on the primary action, key numbers, and rules. The moment it lands on a paragraph it stops meaning 'click here' and starts meaning nothing.

Match the buying emotion

Navy and brass for a decision made out of trust. Safety orange for one made on capability. Cream and burgundy for one made on taste. The palette is a positioning tool.

Found one you like?

Tell me which scheme caught your eye and what your business does — I'll come back with how it would look on your actual pages.

Start a project Email me
